local_names.romanized_local: Zha Fan Shu Bing (Mandarin); Cucur Keledek (Malay)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for battered deep-fried sweet potato fritter; A0FKP (sweet potato, fried) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor, representing a closest parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for sweet potato fritter; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sweet potato fritter in Singapore is one piece weighing approximately 60 g, commonly sold at hawker centres and pasar malam stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Potato Fritter — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sweet potato fritter (deep-fried battered sweet potato), CR ID F11090060. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for deep-fried sweet potato
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for battered deep-fried sweet potato fritter in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 61 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: boiled/baked sweet potato (GI range 44–70, median ~61 for orange-flesh variety, University of Sydney GI Database). Deep-frying with batter is expected to moderately elevate GI relative to boiled sweet potato due to gelatinisation of starch in the batter and disruption of cell-wall fibre structure; the median value of 61 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 18.84) / 100 ≈ 11.5 for a 60 g serving (18.84 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 254 mg/100 g from batter and frying process; high fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sweet potato fritter consumption due to borderline sodium content (254 mg/100 g) and high fat from deep-frying; baked sweet potato without batter is a substantially lower-sodium, lower-fat alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 31.4 g/100 g with medium GI 61; refined batter adds rapidly digestible starch; fat 13.6 g/100 g from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit sweet potato fritter because the combination of medium GI (61), 31.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g, and 13.6 g fat from deep-frying creates a high postprandial glucose and caloric load; baked sweet potato without batter is a significantly better alternative for blood glucose management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 13.6 g/100 g including saturated fat ~2.1 g and trace trans fat 0.1 g from frying oil; sodium 254 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise sweet potato fritter intake due to elevated total fat (13.6 g/100 g), saturated fat (~2.1 g/100 g), trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from frying oil), and borderline sodium (254 mg/100 g), all of which are unfavourable for cardiovascular risk management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid sweet potato fritter because deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from frying oil exacerbates systemic inflammation; baked sweet potato preserves anti-inflammatory beta-carotene without AGE formation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet potato fritter (炸番薯饼; Cucur Keledek) is a popular Singaporean hawker snack consisting of sliced or mashed sweet potato coated in a wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The exterior is crunchy and lightly oily, while the interior is soft, naturally sweet, and slightly fibrous from the sweet potato flesh. It is commonly sold at pasar malam (night markets), hawker centres, and roadside stalls across Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 280 kcal, 31.4 g carbohydrate, 13.6 g fat, 2.8 g protein, 3.9 g dietary fibre, 7.6 g sugar, and 254 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 11.5 per standard 60 g serving. The deep-frying process substantially increases caloric density and fat content relative to baked sweet potato, and generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory. Trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) may be present depending on the frying oil used. Despite the fibre content (3.9 g/100 g) from the sweet potato, the overall nutritional profile is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Sweet potato fritter is best consumed occasionally and in small portions. Baked sweet potato without batter is a nutritionally superior alternative that preserves beta-carotene and eliminates AGE formation.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat sweet potato fritter?
How many calories in a sweet potato fritter?
Is fried sweet potato good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11090060), which is the approved primary regional source for Singaporean foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (closest defensible match: orange-flesh sweet potato, GI ~61); confidence on GI component is medium (0.9 per input). Overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match for all nutrient values.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for sweet potato fritter not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 based on typical frying oil contribution for deep-fried battered snacks; this is an estimate consistent with HPB guidance on deep-fried foods and is not a confirmed zero. potassium_mg set to 200 as an approximate value consistent with sweet potato composition and referenced in the input health_suitability notes; this is an estimate and not a confirmed HPB-reported value. saturated_fat_g of 2.1 g estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for sweet potato, cooked, fried (closest approved secondary source) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for orange-flesh sweet potato; no direct GI measurement for battered deep-fried sweet potato fritter is available in any approved source. Glycemic load per serving = (61 × 18.84) / 100 ≈ 11.5 for a 60 g serving containing 18.84 g carbohydrate. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (sweet potato, fried) used as closest defensible analogous anchor; no exact FoodEx2 code for battered deep-fried sweet potato fritter exists. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302757 used as closest defensible match for sweet potato fritter.
